patient.instructions:

Don’t mention lifestyle limits, water-weight gain, belly bloating, humid/hot shower intolerance, higher resting heart rate, or slow recovery unless directly asked about daily activities, weight, swelling, or triggers.

If asked about associated symptoms, admit: lightheadedness, near-fainting that improves with sitting, exertional chest heaviness relieved by rest, needing extra pillows, occasional waking at night short of breath, worsening leg swelling/tight shoes, rare bluish lips/fingers when very winded, cough with tiny blood streaks.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of insomnia as the primary diagnosis after appropriate clinical evaluation.

nl_assertions: Uses the term "insomnia" explicitly when explaining the diagnosis to the patient.

Explains first-line treatment as CBT-I (sleep hygiene, stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive strategies) and frames medications as secondary/short-term options (sedative-hypnotics) when appropriate.
Assesses core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, duration/frequency, and daytime impairment.

Screens for common contributors and red flags that would change management: depression/anxiety, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, medication-induced insomnia, pain, restless legs symptoms, and symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness).
Clarifies sleep schedule and behaviors (bedtime/wake time, time in bed, naps, shift work, screen use) and provides at least two concrete behavioral recommendations consistent with CBT-I.

Does not prescribe or recommend sedative-hypnotics without first completing critical safety checks: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check (e.g., pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment depending on agent, history of substance use disorder, untreated sleep apnea, high fall risk).

If sedative-hypnotics are discussed, communicates key safety counseling: lowest effective dose, shortest duration, avoid alcohol/other sedatives, next-day impairment/driving risk, complex sleep behaviors, and dependence/tolerance risks.
Provides a clear follow-up timeframe and criteria for escalation (persistent symptoms despite CBT-I, suspected OSA/RLS, significant psychiatric comorbidity, safety concerns).
